# dcode-ai

Rust-native coding agent. Single binary. Terminal-first.

Install · Quick Start · Commands · Interactive UX · Providers · Funding

--- `dcode-ai` is a Rust-native coding CLI that ships as a single binary. It is built for local-first, terminal-first workflows: interactive TUI, line REPL, one-shot runs, detached sessions, attach/status/logs, JSON and NDJSON output, Unix-socket IPC, worktree-isolated subagents, and autonomous research helpers. It is meant for people who like their AI tooling close to the terminal: fast to start, easy to script, and capable of running real session workflows without dragging in a browser shell. The product surface is the CLI. No desktop wrapper, no Electron, no browser in the default path. ## What It Does - Runs coding tasks in an interactive TUI or a line-oriented REPL. - Supports one-shot runs and detached background sessions. - Persists session state and event logs under the current workspace. - Exposes machine-readable JSON and NDJSON for automation. - Spawns child agents with explicit parent/child lineage and optional git worktrees. - Uses MiniMax by default, with OpenAI, Anthropic, and OpenRouter support. - Loads built-in tools plus optional MCP tools from config. - Sends **native multimodal** (text + image) messages to MiniMax and other vision-capable models. - Auto-summarizes long conversations to prevent token overflow. - Discovers skills from `AGENTS.md` sections, filesystem directories, and user-level skill directories. - Builds a cached CLI index for workspace-aware agent context. ## Why People Reach For It - You want a coding CLI that feels quick and stays out of the way. - You want sessions, event logs, and resumable work instead of a throwaway prompt box. - You want child agents that can branch off cleanly with lineage and optional git worktrees. - You want a CLI that still works well when another system is driving it through JSON, NDJSON, and IPC. - You want intelligent context management that auto-summarizes without losing important context. ## Common Use Cases | Use case | Why `dcode-ai` fits | |---|---| | Solo coding in the terminal | Start with `dcode-ai`, use the TUI, switch agent profiles, review diffs, and keep everything in one terminal-native flow. | | Quick one-shot work | `dcode-ai run --prompt ...` gives you a focused foreground task without opening a longer session than you need. | | Background analysis | `dcode-ai spawn --prompt ...` lets you kick off work, keep coding, then come back with `status`, `logs`, or `attach`. | | Multi-agent exploration | Parent and child sessions keep lineage, and child runs can use separate git worktrees for isolation. | | Automation and orchestration | `--json`, `--stream ndjson`, Unix-socket IPC, and `DCODE_AI_ORCH_*` metadata make it usable as a worker process. | | Long-running research | `dcode-ai autoresearch once` runs metric-driven experiments with parsed output for CI/profiling. | ## Installation ### One-line install (macOS and Linux) ```bash curl -fsSL https://dcode-ai-cli.com/install | bash ``` This detects your platform, downloads the latest release from GitHub, and installs `dcode-ai` to `/usr/local/bin`. Set `DCODE_AI_INSTALL_DIR` to change the install path. ### GitHub Releases Pre-built binaries for every release are available on the [Releases](https://github.com/madebyaris/dcode-ai/releases) page: | Platform | Target | |---|---| | macOS (Apple Silicon) | `aarch64-apple-darwin` | | macOS (Intel) | `x86_64-apple-darwin` | | Linux (x86_64) | `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu` | ### Build from source Requires Rust edition 2024 (use a recent toolchain). ```bash git clone https://github.com/madebyaris/dcode-ai.git cd dcode-ai cargo build --release cp target/release/dcode-ai /usr/local/bin/ ``` ## Quick Start ```bash # Configure a provider export MINIMAX_API_KEY="your-api-key" # Start the interactive CLI dcode-ai # Line REPL instead of the full-screen TUI dcode-ai --no-tui # Run one task and exit dcode-ai run --prompt "Explain this repository" # Spawn a detached session dcode-ai spawn --prompt "Inspect the repo and draft a plan" # Inspect and attach dcode-ai sessions dcode-ai status dcode-ai attach ``` The full-screen UI appears when `stdin` and `stdout` are TTYs and `--stream human` is active. Otherwise `dcode-ai` falls back to the line-oriented REPL or one-shot execution path. ### Images (full-screen TUI) In the default TUI you can attach images for the **next** user message: - **Ctrl+V** — paste a bitmap from the system clipboard (saved as PNG under the session). - **`/image paste`** — same as clipboard paste if Ctrl+V is not available. - **`/image path/to/screenshot.png`** — copy a file into the session attachment dir. - **`/image clear`** — remove staged images before you press Enter. For **MiniMax**, pasted images are analyzed with the same HTTP API as the MCP's `understand_image` tool (`POST /v1/coding_plan/vlm` on `https://api.minimax.io` or your region host); dcode-ai does this in Rust (no Python MCP). The description is merged into the user message before `/v1/messages`. Other providers use their own multimodal chat formats where supported. If the **selected provider/model is not treated as vision-capable**, `dcode-ai` **errors** instead of silently dropping images. Session attachment copies are removed automatically after a successful send/process; your original source file is not deleted. ## A Quick Look The main interface is designed to feel like a serious terminal tool, not a toy overlay. ![dcode-ai interactive view](docs/images/dcode-ai-show.png) ## Core Commands | Command | Purpose | |---|---| | `dcode-ai` | Start the default interactive experience. Auto-resumes the last session unless `--no-resume` is used. | | `dcode-ai run --prompt ...` | Run one task in the foreground. | | `dcode-ai spawn --prompt ...` | Start a detached session and return immediately. | | `dcode-ai resume ` | Resume a saved session. | | `dcode-ai attach ` | Attach to a running session over IPC. | | `dcode-ai logs ` | Read or follow the event log. | | `dcode-ai status ` | Show session status and metadata. | | `dcode-ai cancel ` | Mark a detached session as cancelled. | | `dcode-ai sessions` | List saved sessions, with filters like `--status`, `--since-hours`, and `--search`. | | `dcode-ai models` | Show configured models and provider-facing defaults. | | `dcode-ai doctor` | Check provider readiness, skills, and memory/config paths. | | `dcode-ai config` | Print effective config and resolved paths. | | `dcode-ai memory list\|add` | Inspect or append workspace memory notes. | | `dcode-ai skills` | List discovered skills with their source (`AGENTS.md`, filesystem, or user directory). | | `dcode-ai mcp` | List configured MCP servers. | | `dcode-ai completion ` | Generate shell completions. | | `dcode-ai index build\|show` | Build or inspect a cached CLI index under `~/.dcode-ai/workspaces//`. | | `dcode-ai autoresearch once ` | Run a metric-driven research program and print parsed output. | There is also a hidden `serve` subcommand used for IPC-oriented service sessions. ## Interactive UX The interactive surface has two modes: - Full-screen TUI with transcript, composer, approvals, structured questions, slash-command palette, session sidebar, and branch picker. - Line-oriented REPL built on `reedline` for scripts, terminals where TUI is not desired, or cases where `--no-tui` is easier. Useful interactive behaviors: - `! ` runs a shell command. - `@ ` searches files (fuzzy file mention completions). - `/...` runs slash commands. - `Tab` cycles agent profiles such as `build`, `plan`, `review`, `fix`, and `test`. - `Ctrl+C` or `/stop` cancels the current running turn. - `/auto-answer` accepts the suggested answer for a pending `ask_question`. Small touches in the TUI matter too: branch switching, structured options, session sidebars, model picker, provider configuration, and direct control over long-running turns. ![branch picker](docs/images/git-branch.png) ![interactive options](docs/images/option.png) ## Output and Automation `dcode-ai` is designed to work well in two very different moods: terminal-first for humans, and machine-friendly for orchestrators. - `--stream off` returns only the final output. - `--stream human` renders the normal terminal experience. - `--stream ndjson` emits newline-delimited event envelopes. - `--json` is available on lifecycle-oriented commands such as `spawn`, `sessions`, `status`, `cancel`, `skills`, `models`, `doctor`, `config`, `index show`, and `mcp`. - `DCODE_AI_ORCH_*` and `DCODE_AI_ORCH_META_*` environment variables attach orchestration metadata to sessions and harness context. See [Orchestration Contract](docs/orchestration.md) for the subprocess-facing surface. ## Storage and Paths `dcode-ai` is workspace-first. The current workspace keeps its own session history and local state. | Path | Purpose | |---|---| | `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml` | Global config file. | | `/.dcode-ai/config.local.toml` | Workspace-local config overrides. | | `/.dcode-ai/sessions/.json` | Saved session state. | | `/.dcode-ai/sessions/.events.jsonl` | Event log for the session. | | `/.dcode-ai/memory.json` | Default memory store. | | `/AGENTS.md` | Repo-local instruction layer; each `## Heading` is also a discoverable skill. | | `/.dcode-ai/skills/` | Default workspace skill directory. | | `~/.dcode-ai/skills/` | User-level skill directory. | | `~/.claude/skills/` | Imported Claude-style skill directory, if present. | | `/.dcode-ai/worktrees/` | Worktree path for isolated child sessions. | | `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/dcode-ai/.sock` | IPC socket path when `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` is set. | | `/tmp/dcode-ai/.sock` | IPC socket fallback when `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` is not set. | | `~/.dcode-ai/workspaces//cli-index.json` | Cached CLI index for agents and tooling. | | `.dcode-airc` | Project instructions file committed with the repo. | | `.dcode-ai/instructions.md` | Local instructions file. | ## Skills System `dcode-ai` discovers skills from multiple sources with visible provenance: 1. **`AGENTS.md`** — Each root-level `## Heading` becomes a slash-invokable skill. Optional directive bullets can set `model=...`, `permission_mode=...`, and `context=...`. 2. **Filesystem directories** — Skills from `.dcode-ai/skills/`, `~/.dcode-ai/skills/`, and `~/.claude/skills/`. 3. **Built-in skills** — Core skills baked into the binary. Use `dcode-ai skills --json` to see all discovered skills with their sources. ## Context Management `dcode-ai` automatically manages conversation context to prevent token overflow: - **Token estimation** uses character-based approximation (chars / 4, with tool-message adjustments). - **Auto-summarize** kicks in when context reaches a configurable threshold (default 75%). - **Summary format** preserves key topics, decisions, and critical context. - System messages are always preserved; recent messages use a sliding window. Configuration in `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml`: ```toml [memory.context] context_window_target = 32000 max_retained_messages = 50 auto_summarize_threshold = 75 enable_auto_summarize = true ``` ## Providers MiniMax is the default provider path. The codebase also supports OpenAI, Anthropic, and OpenRouter, so the project can stay MiniMax-first without boxing itself into one provider forever. Typical environment variables: - `MINIMAX_API_KEY` - `OPENAI_API_KEY` - `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` - `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` Provider config is loaded from defaults, then `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml`, then `/.dcode-ai/config.local.toml`, then environment overrides. Use `dcode-ai doctor` to verify provider readiness and `dcode-ai models` to inspect model selection. ## Harness and Tooling The system prompt is layered in this order: 1. Built-in harness prompt 2. Permission-mode guidance 3. Project guidance from `AGENTS.md` (full file as instructions) 4. Project instructions from `.dcode-airc` 5. Local instructions from `.dcode-ai/instructions.md` 6. Discovered skills summary 7. Orchestration context The built-in tool surface includes filesystem editing, search, diffing, patching, shell execution, web access, `ask_question`, and `spawn_subagent`. MCP tools are loaded dynamically when configured, so the available tool set can grow with your environment. ### Search And Edit Tools Recent search/edit improvements are aimed at making agent file work less brittle: - `search_code` now returns structured JSON match objects instead of raw `rg` text. - `search_code` treats ripgrep exit code `1` as a successful empty result, not a failure. - `search_code` supports `path`, `glob`, `fixed_strings`, `case_sensitive`, `word`, `context_before`, `context_after`, and `max_results`. - `query_symbols` is a literal Rust symbol lookup, not an implicit regex expansion of user input. - `edit_file` and `apply_patch` now fail loudly on ambiguous single-match edits instead of silently changing the first occurrence. - `replace_match` can edit a specific search result by exact `path`, `line`, and `column`, which makes search -> edit flows much safer. ## Crate Layout | Crate | Responsibility | |---|---| | `crates/common` | Shared config, events, sessions, messages, tool schemas, and orchestration metadata. | | `crates/core` | Agent loop, provider abstraction, harness builder, skills, approvals, and tool registry. | | `crates/runtime` | Session supervision, IPC, persistence, worktrees, memory store, context management, and subagent execution. | | `crates/cli` | `dcode-ai` entrypoint, command parsing, stream rendering, REPL, and TUI. | | `crates/autoresearch` | Metric-driven autonomous research helpers and experiment runner. | ## Session Model - Sessions are persisted as JSON snapshots plus JSONL event logs. - The runtime uses a `Supervisor` to own lifecycle, IPC, approvals, questions, event fanout, and persistence. - Child sessions can inherit parent context, record lineage in session metadata, and run inside separate git worktrees. - IPC uses newline-delimited JSON over Unix sockets so `attach`, approvals, status, and other controls share one runtime transport. - `ContextManager` tracks token usage and auto-summarizes long conversations. In practice, that means you can start small, branch out when a task gets bigger, and still keep a clean trail of what happened. ## Documentation Full user-facing documentation lives in [`docs/documentation/`](docs/documentation/index.md): | Page | Description | |---|---| | [Getting Started](docs/documentation/getting-started.md) | Installation, first run, and initial configuration | | [Commands](docs/documentation/commands.md) | Complete CLI command and flag reference | | [Interactive Mode](docs/documentation/interactive-mode.md) | TUI, REPL, slash commands, keyboard shortcuts | | [Configuration](docs/documentation/configuration.md) | Config files, TOML format, and environment variables | | [Providers](docs/documentation/providers.md) | LLM provider setup — MiniMax, Anthropic, OpenAI, OpenRouter | | [Tools](docs/documentation/tools.md) | All agent tools — file ops, search, shell, web, and more | | [Sessions](docs/documentation/sessions.md) | Session lifecycle, persistence, resume, and management | | [Permissions](docs/documentation/permissions.md) | Approval system, permission modes, and safe mode | | [Skills](docs/documentation/skills.md) | Skill discovery, installation, and authoring | | [Advanced](docs/documentation/advanced.md) | Sub-agents, MCP servers, hooks, orchestration, and IPC | Internal design docs: - [Product Requirements](docs/prd.md) - [Tech Stack](docs/tech-stack.md) - [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) - [Orchestration Contract](docs/orchestration.md) - [Context Management](docs/context-management.md) - [Performance Optimization Research](docs/research/rust-ratatui-optimization.md) ## Funding `dcode-ai` is an independent project built by [Aris Setia](https://github.com/madebyaris). It is not backed by a company and is not powered by any single provider — the multi-provider architecture is intentional so users can choose what works for them. MiniMax has supported this work through content collaboration and developer events in Indonesia, which helped make the early development possible. Sustaining full-time work on the Rust-native AI ecosystem requires ongoing support. If you find `dcode-ai` useful and want to help keep it going: | Channel | Link | |---|---| | GitHub Sponsors | [github.com/madebyaris](https://github.com/madebyaris) | | PayPal | [paypal.me/airs](https://paypal.me/airs) (arissetia.m@gmail.com) | Your support directly funds full-time development on `dcode-ai` and the broader Native CLI ecosystem — better provider support, performance work, new tools, and documentation. ## License MIT

## Learned User Preferences

- Always use Rust-native solutions; no JavaScript, Node.js, or Electron involvement
- MiniMax M2.5 is the primary LLM provider; prioritize MiniMax integration over other providers
- **CLI (`dcode-ai`) is the product surface**—terminal UX, JSON/NDJSON streams, and Unix-socket IPC
- Empty provider completions must fail loudly, never silently succeed
- Use plan-first workflow: create a plan document, then implement from it
- Install CLI via `cargo build --release` then `cp target/release/dcode-ai /usr/local/bin/`
- Keep each crate focused: `common` for shared types, `core` for agent logic, `runtime` for session lifecycle, `cli` for terminal UX
- User tests CLI in separate workspace directories (e.g. `test-makan`, `for-test`)
- Prefer efficient algorithms and fast execution; the CLI is intended as a spawner for heavy tasks
- Sub-agents should use isolated worktrees; parent/child session lineage should stay visible in session metadata and events

## Learned Workspace Facts

- Rust workspace with **4 crates**: `dcode-ai-common`, `dcode-ai-core`, `dcode-ai-runtime`, `dcode-ai-cli`
- IPC between CLI and runtime uses Unix domain sockets with newline-delimited JSON
- Sessions persisted as `<id>.json` (state) + `<id>.events.jsonl` (event log) in `<workspace>/.dcode-ai/sessions/`
- MiniMax provider endpoint: `https://api.minimaxi.chat/v1/text/chatcompletion_v2`
- Global config at `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml`
- Git worktrees for isolated agent runs stored at `<repo>/.dcode-ai/worktrees/<session-id>`
- **Single shipped app binary:** `dcode-ai` (CLI)
- Tokio async runtime; `async-trait` for tool executor and approval handler interfaces
- Session lineage: parent/child session IDs, inherited summary, spawn reason tracked in `SessionMeta`
- `AgentEvent` enum is the shared event bus for CLI rendering, IPC broadcast, and disk persistence
- Runtime socket dir defaults to `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/dcode-ai/` or `/tmp/dcode-ai/`

event.content:
This project is **`dcode-ai`**: a **Rust-native AI coding agent CLI**. You install/build **one binary** called `dcode-ai`, and you use it from the terminal to run “sessions” where an LLM (by default **MiniMax**) helps you inspect code, edit files, run commands, and keep a persistent log of what happened.

## What it does (beginner view)
Think of it like a terminal app with:
- An **interactive mode** (full-screen TUI or a simpler line REPL)
- A **one-shot mode** (`run`) to do one task and exit
- A **detached/background mode** (`spawn`) so you can start a long task and come back later
- A **session system** so your work is saved and resumable (not just a chat prompt)

## The big idea: “sessions” + “events”
When you start `dcode-ai`, it creates (or resumes) a **session**. A session is stored in your current repo/workspace under:

- `<workspace>/.dcode-ai/sessions/<id>.json` → current session state snapshot  
- `<workspace>/.dcode-ai/sessions/<id>.events.jsonl` → append-only event log (every message/tool/action)

That event log is also what powers:
- `dcode-ai logs <id>` (view/follow what happened)
- `dcode-ai attach <id>` (attach to a running session via IPC)

## How it works internally (still beginner-friendly)
There are 4 main Rust crates (modules) in `crates/`:

- **`crates/common`**: shared types/config/events (stuff everyone needs)
- **`crates/core`**: the “agent brain loop” + provider API layer (MiniMax/OpenAI/etc) + tool protocol
- **`crates/runtime`**: session lifecycle (save/load), IPC sockets, supervision, worktrees, context/summaries
- **`crates/cli`**: the user interface (commands, TUI/REPL rendering, human vs JSON/NDJSON output)

So, conceptually:
1. **CLI** parses your command and starts/resumes a session
2. **Runtime** supervises the session (keeps it alive, saves it, streams events)
3. **Core** talks to the LLM provider (MiniMax by default) and decides which “tools” to call
4. Tools do real work: read/search/edit files, run shell commands, etc.
5. Everything becomes **events** that get shown in the UI and written to disk

## Providers (where the AI comes from)
MiniMax is the default/primary provider path. You typically set:

```bash
export MINIMAX_API_KEY="..."
```

Config is loaded in layers:
1. defaults
2. `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml`
3. `<workspace>/.dcode-ai/config.local.toml`
4. environment variables

To check if you’re set up correctly:
- `dcode-ai doctor`
- `dcode-ai models`

## IPC + attach (why it feels “app-like”)
Detached sessions communicate over a **Unix domain socket** (newline-delimited JSON). That’s how:
- `attach`, approvals, status updates, etc. work in real time.

Socket location is usually:
- `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/dcode-ai/` or fallback `/tmp/dcode-ai/`

## Sub-agents (child sessions) + worktrees
It can spawn **child agents** as separate sessions, optionally in **separate git worktrees** under:
- `<repo>/.dcode-ai/worktrees/<session-id>`

This helps keep experiments isolated while still recording parent/child lineage in metadata.

## How you would use it (quick mental model)
- “I want help now, interactively”: `dcode-ai`
- “Do one thing and exit”: `dcode-ai run --prompt "Explain this repo"`
- “Work in background”: `dcode-ai spawn --prompt "..."` then later `dcode-ai attach <id>`

If you tell me how you want to use it (interactive vs scripts vs background), I can point you to the best first command and the minimum config to set.

Hi—what do you want to do with `dcode-ai` today?

If you tell me one of these, I can jump in quickly:
- add/adjust a MiniMax provider feature
- fix a bug (share the error/log)
- improve CLI UX/NDJSON/IPC behavior
- implement a new tool or agent capability
Hi. What do you want to do with `dcode-ai` today—run a session, debug something, or implement a feature?
Hi. What do you want to do in this repo—build/run `dcode-ai`, debug something, or implement a feature?
what is the current project explain me

This repo is a Rust workspace that builds a single CLI app: **`dcode-ai`**. The CLI is the product surface (terminal UX + JSON/NDJSON streams), and it talks to a local runtime over a **Unix domain socket**.

## What `dcode-ai` does (high level)
- Runs “agent sessions” that use LLM providers to do work (chat, tool execution, approvals, etc.).
- Sessions are **persisted locally** under a per-workspace directory:  
  `./.dcode-ai/sessions/`
  - `<id>.json` = session state/metadata  
  - `<id>.events.jsonl` = append-only event log (NDJSON)
- Supports multiple LLM providers (notably **MiniMax** as a first-class endpoint, plus OpenAI / Anthropic / OpenRouter / Antigravity).
- Uses **git worktrees** to isolate agent runs:  
  `./.dcode-ai/worktrees/<session-id>/`  
  This keeps each session’s edits separate and traceable.
- Has an approval/attach flow via IPC so the CLI can ask a user to approve actions or attach context.

## Workspace layout (4 crates, strict responsibilities)
1. **`dcode-ai-common`**
   - Shared types and configuration.
   - Things like config loading (`~/.dcode-ai/config.toml`), shared enums/structs used by all crates.

2. **`dcode-ai-core`**
   - The “agent brain”: provider integrations, tool protocol, harness, agent logic.
   - Where LLM requests/responses are handled and normalized.

3. **`dcode-ai-runtime`**
   - Manages session lifecycle: start/stop, persistence, supervision.
   - Owns IPC server over Unix sockets and broadcasts events.
   - Manages worktrees and parent/child session lineage (subagents).

4. **`dcode-ai-cli`**
   - Terminal UX only.
   - Connects to runtime over the socket, renders events, and sends user inputs/approvals.

## Key design constraints
- **Rust-native only** (no JS/web wrappers).
- **Empty provider completions must error loudly** (no silent “success”).
- Subagents are real child sessions with their own worktrees and visible lineage.

If you tell me what angle you want—“how to run it”, “how sessions and IPC work”, or “where to add a new provider/command”—I can walk you through that part specifically.

event.content: │- Has an approval/attach flow via IPC so the CLI can ask a user to approve actions or      │

I can read images, but I **can’t access** `/mnt/d/...` directly from your machine path. You need to **attach** the screenshot in this chat (using the CLI’s image attach / paste-image feature), or paste the text from it.

That line you pasted means:

- The **runtime** and **CLI** communicate over a **Unix socket IPC channel**.
- When the agent wants to do something that needs confirmation (e.g., write files, run a command, delete/move things), it emits an **approval request** event.
- The **CLI** renders that request to you and sends your **approve/deny** response back to the runtime over IPC.
- “attach flow” similarly means the CLI can send **attachments/context** (like files or images you provide) to the running session via IPC.

If you attach the screenshot here, I can explain exactly what that part of the UI/log is showing and what action it’s asking for.

That “attach context” line is saying the CLI can send extra **context** into an active agent session, via the IPC channel to the runtime.

In concrete terms, “context” can be things like:
- a file you want the agent to read,
- a snippet of text/log output,
- an image (if supported in your flow),
- or other session inputs that aren’t just normal chat messages.

Typical flow:
1. Agent session is running in the runtime.
2. You use the CLI to **attach** something (or it prompts you).
3. CLI uploads/sends a reference to that attachment over the Unix socket.
4. Runtime records it in the session/event log, and the core agent can use it.

That line is the start of a section explaining how the Rust workspace is separated into **4 crates**, each with a narrow, explicit responsibility. The point is: when you want to change something, you should change it in the correct crate (so the architecture stays clean).

Here’s what that section means:

- **`dcode-ai-common`**  
  Shared stuff used everywhere: config types, shared enums/structs, helpers that are not “agent logic” or “UI”.

- **`dcode-ai-core`**  
  The “brain”: agent loop, tool protocol, and LLM provider implementations (OpenAI/Anthropic/OpenRouter/MiniMax/…).

- **`dcode-ai-runtime`**  
  The “daemon/session manager”: creates and supervises sessions, persists state + events to `./.dcode-ai/sessions/`, manages worktrees, runs the IPC server (Unix socket) the CLI connects to.

- **`dcode-ai-cli`**  
  Terminal app only: parses commands/flags, connects to runtime via IPC, renders session events and prompts you for approvals/inputs.

If you tell me what you want to modify (example: “add a new CLI command”, “change where sessions are stored”, “add a provider”, “change approval prompts”), I’ll point you to the exact crate + files to start with.

`1. dcode-ai-common` means the first crate in the workspace is **`dcode-ai-common`**, and its job is to hold **shared types and config** that multiple parts of the system need.

What you typically put in `dcode-ai-common`:
- **Configuration**: loading/validating config from `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml` (and any per-project overrides if supported).
- **Shared data models**: structs/enums used across CLI/runtime/core, like:
  - provider settings (API keys, base URLs, model names),
  - session metadata types,
  - event/message types (if they’re shared),
  - error types that need to cross crate boundaries.
- **Utility code** that is truly shared and not UI-specific or runtime-specific.

What you typically *don’t* put there:
- CLI rendering, prompts, terminal formatting → that belongs in `dcode-ai-cli`.
- Provider HTTP calls / agent loop logic → that belongs in `dcode-ai-core`.
- Session persistence, IPC server, worktrees → that belongs in `dcode-ai-runtime`.

If you want, I can point to the exact folders/files in `dcode-ai-common` in this repo (Cargo.toml + `src/` structure) if you run `ls dcode-ai-common/src` and paste it, or I can inspect it directly if you want me to.

That bullet means **`dcode-ai-common`** is where the project keeps code that is used by *more than one* crate:

- **Shared types**: Rust `struct`s/`enum`s that need to be understood the same way by CLI, runtime, and core.  
  Examples: config structs, provider/model identifiers, session IDs/metadata, event enums, request/response shapes that cross IPC boundaries.

- **Shared configuration**: code and types for reading/writing/validating settings, typically from:
  - `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml` (global user config)
  - (optionally) repo/workspace-level config if supported

Why it matters: if something must be consistent across the whole app (e.g., how a session is identified, how a provider is configured), it goes in **common** so you don’t duplicate it in multiple crates.

That line is giving examples of what “shared types and configuration” means in practice:

- **Config loading**: the code that reads your user config file at  
  `~/.dcode-ai/config.toml`  
  This typically includes:
  - locating the file (respecting OS conventions / `$HOME`)
  - parsing TOML into Rust structs (usually via `serde`)
  - default values + validation (e.g., “API key missing”)

- **Shared enums/structs used by all**: data types that multiple crates need to agree on, such as:
  - `Provider` / `ProviderKind` enum (OpenAI / Anthropic / OpenRouter / MiniMax / …)
  - provider credentials/settings structs (api_key, base_url, model)
  - session identity types (`SessionId`, `SessionMeta`)
  - event types (`AgentEvent`) if CLI + runtime + core all need to read/write them

The key idea: **the same config and data structures are reused** across:
- `dcode-ai-cli` (to show config errors / select provider / render events),
- `dcode-ai-runtime` (to start sessions, persist metadata, serve IPC),
- `dcode-ai-core` (to make provider calls and run the agent loop).

`2. dcode-ai-core` is the second crate, and it’s where the **agent logic** lives—basically the “brain” that decides what to do and how to talk to LLM providers.

What typically lives in `dcode-ai-core`:

- **Provider integrations**
  - Code that builds HTTP requests to providers (OpenAI / Anthropic / OpenRouter / MiniMax / etc.)
  - Normalizes responses into a common internal format
  - Handles streaming, retries, rate limits, and provider-specific quirks

- **Agent loop / harness**
  - The main loop that takes the current session state + user input
  - Calls the model
  - Interprets tool calls / function calls
  - Produces events (messages, tool results, errors) that the runtime persists and the CLI renders

- **Tool protocol**
  - Definitions for “tools” the agent can run (read/write files, run commands, search, etc.)
  - The request/response types and dispatch logic
  - Error handling rules (and importantly: empty/invalid model output should fail loudly)

What *doesn’t* belong here:
- IPC socket server and session persistence → `dcode-ai-runtime`
- Terminal UI rendering / prompts → `dcode-ai-cli`
- Pure shared config/types → `dcode-ai-common`

If you tell me what you want to add/change (e.g., “add a new provider”, “change tool behavior”, “improve streaming”), I can point you to the exact module(s) inside `dcode-ai-core` to edit.
│- The “agent brain”: provider integrations, tool protocol, harness, agent logic.           │

That bullet means `dcode-ai-core` contains the code that actually *runs the agent*.

Breakdown of the terms:

- **“agent brain”**: the decision-making loop. It takes inputs (your prompt + session context), asks an LLM, and decides next actions (reply, call tools, ask for approval, spawn subagent, etc.).

- **provider integrations**: adapters for each LLM provider (MiniMax/OpenAI/Anthropic/OpenRouter/…). They handle:
  - building the provider-specific request payload,
  - auth headers / API keys,
  - parsing the response,
  - streaming output if supported,
  - translating provider output into the project’s internal message/event format.

- **tool protocol**: the standardized way the model requests actions (“call tool X with args Y”) and how the system returns results/errors. This includes:
  - tool schemas / argument parsing,
  - dispatch to actual tool implementations (often executed by runtime),
  - strict error handling (don’t accept empty/bad tool calls).

- **harness**: the “container” around the agent loop that wires everything together:
  - connects provider client + tool executor + session state,
  - emits structured events,
  - applies policy (approvals needed, limits, etc.).

- **agent logic**: the higher-level rules: when to call tools, when to ask follow-ups, how to summarize, how to manage context windows, etc.

If you want, I can locate the main entry point in `dcode-ai-core` (the function that starts the agent loop) and outline the call flow.
│- Where LLM requests/responses are handled and normalized.                                 │

That line means:

- `dcode-ai-core` is responsible for **sending requests to the LLM provider** (build the payload, add auth, choose model, set temperature/max tokens, streaming options, etc.).
- It also **receives the provider’s raw response** (which differs per provider) and **normalizes** it into a **common internal format** so the rest of the system (runtime/CLI/tools) doesn’t need to care which provider was used.

“Normalized” typically includes converting provider-specific fields into shared concepts like:
- assistant text output,
- tool/function calls (name + JSON args),
- usage/token counts,
- finish reason (stop/length/tool_call/error),
- structured errors.

Why this matters: with normalization, swapping providers (MiniMax vs OpenAI vs Anthropic) doesn’t require rewriting the agent loop—only the provider adapter changes.

If you want to see the exact structs used for that normalization in this repo, tell me “show me the normalized response types” and I’ll pull up the relevant code.

That blank line is just formatting in the boxed output you’re viewing.

If you want to move from the explanation text to the actual implementation, tell me which area to inspect first and I’ll open the relevant files in the repo:

- provider request/response normalization (in `dcode-ai-core`)
- session persistence + IPC (in `dcode-ai-runtime`)
- CLI rendering of events/approvals (in `dcode-ai-cli`)
- config types + loader (in `dcode-ai-common`)

`3. dcode-ai-runtime` is the crate that runs the “runtime” side of the app: it manages **sessions**, **persistence**, **IPC**, and **worktrees**. Think of it as the local supervisor/daemon that the CLI talks to.

What lives in `dcode-ai-runtime`:

- **Session lifecycle**
  - create/start/stop sessions
  - maintain session metadata (including parent/child when spawning subagents)
  - coordinate the agent execution (often by calling into `dcode-ai-core`)

- **Persistence**
  - writes session state to `./.dcode-ai/sessions/<id>.json`
  - appends events to `./.dcode-ai/sessions/<id>.events.jsonl` (NDJSON event log)

- **IPC server**
  - listens on a **Unix domain socket**
  - accepts connections from `dcode-ai` CLI
  - streams events to clients and receives commands/approvals/attachments from them

- **Worktree management**
  - creates isolated git worktrees per session under `./.dcode-ai/worktrees/<session-id>/`
  - ensures file edits/runs happen in the right isolated directory

What it should *not* contain:
- terminal UI formatting → `dcode-ai-cli`
- provider HTTP adapters and LLM normalization → `dcode-ai-core`
- generic shared config types → `dcode-ai-common`

If you tell me what you’re trying to do (e.g., “where are sessions stored?”, “how does IPC work?”, “how are worktrees created?”), I’ll point you to the exact module(s) in `dcode-ai-runtime`.
│- Manages session lifecycle: start/stop, persistence, supervision.                         │

That bullet means `dcode-ai-runtime` is the part of the system responsible for **running and controlling sessions over time**, not just “thinking” (core) or “displaying” (CLI).

Specifically:

- **start/stop**: it can create a new session, resume an existing one, and shut it down cleanly (including cleaning up tasks/resources).

- **persistence**: it saves what happened so sessions aren’t ephemeral:
  - session state/metadata to `./.dcode-ai/sessions/<id>.json`
  - an append-only event log to `./.dcode-ai/sessions/<id>.events.jsonl`

- **supervision**: it “watches” the running session:
  - runs async tasks (Tokio) that drive the agent loop
  - handles crashes/errors and reports them as events
  - manages child sessions (subagents) and keeps lineage info
  - coordinates approvals/attachments coming from the CLI via IPC

In short: the runtime is the **local orchestrator/daemon** that keeps everything consistent and recoverable while the agent is running.
│- Owns IPC server over Unix sockets and broadcasts events.                                 │

event.content: │- Empty provider completions must error loudly (no silent “success”).                      │

Those bullets are summarizing what the remaining crates do and the non-negotiable rules of the project:

## `dcode-ai-runtime`
- **Owns IPC server over Unix sockets and broadcasts events**
  - The runtime listens on a **Unix domain socket** (in `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/dcode-ai/` or `/tmp/dcode-ai/`).
  - The CLI connects to it and receives a stream of **events** (typically NDJSON / JSON lines).
  - “Broadcasts events” means multiple clients can subscribe (e.g., another terminal can “attach” to the same running session and see the same event stream).

- **Manages worktrees and parent/child session lineage (subagents)**
  - Each session gets an isolated **git worktree** at `./.dcode-ai/worktrees/<session-id>/`.
  - When spawning subagents, the runtime creates a **child session** (new ID, new worktree) and records lineage: parent ID, spawn reason, inherited summary, etc.

## `dcode-ai-cli`
- **Terminal UX only**
  - It should not contain agent logic or provider code.
  - It’s responsible for parsing commands, displaying output, and collecting user input.

- **Connects to runtime over the socket, renders events, and sends user inputs/approvals**
  - It renders the runtime’s event stream (messages, tool calls/results, errors).
  - When the runtime asks “Approve this?” the CLI prompts you and sends back approve/deny over IPC.

## Key design constraints
- **Rust-native only**
  - No Node/Electron/web UI; everything stays in Rust + terminal + sockets.

- **Empty provider completions must error loudly**
  - If an LLM provider returns an empty response (or something unusable), the system must treat it as an **error** and surface it clearly (event/log), not pretend everything is fine.

If you want, tell me what you’re trying to do next (build/run it, attach to a running session, add a provider, debug IPC), and I’ll guide you step-by-step.
